key_issues: CRITICAL: The spec's flagship 'Exact expected provide form' for mcp/core/types/main.rkt (item.md lines 182-190) does not compile. Live-verified via module->exports: spec-2025-11-25.rkt and spec-2026-07-28.rkt collide on 749 identifiers (shared struct/accessor/json-codec/contract names like prompt-title, tool/c, json->prompt, struct:prompt). A plain unprefixed (require "spec-2025-11-25.rkt" "spec-2026-07-28.rkt") fails with 'module: identifier already required', not the 'all-from-out: name clashes' error the spec spends three paragraphs preparing the implementer for. except-out cannot fix this (it only filters provide, not require-level collisions); the fix needs prefix-in on the require, mirroring the pattern types.rkt itself already uses (types.rkt:31-32) but which this spec never generalizes from.

CRITICAL: The spec's naming-collision analysis (lines 139-163) only checks types.rkt vs spec-2025-11-25.rkt/spec-2026-07-28.rkt (9-10 colliding names, confirmed accurate) but never checks spec-2025-11-25.rkt vs spec-2026-07-28.rkt against EACH OTHER, missing the 749-name collision that is the actual blocker (see issue above). The collision analysis is accurate as far as it goes but dangerously incomplete.

MAJOR: The exact module->imports/module-path-index-resolve transitive walk specified verbatim in Testing strategy Part 2 has a reproducible bug: relative sub-requires (e.g. errors.rkt's (require "types/constants.rkt" ...)) resolve against the process CWD instead of the requiring module's own directory, producing nonexistent paths (e.g. /home/tlam/racket-mcp/types/constants.rkt instead of /home/tlam/racket-mcp/mcp/core/types/constants.rkt). Live-verified three ways including forcing module-path-index-resolve to load the bogus path, which crashes with open-input-file: cannot open module file.

MAJOR: The spec's mandated with-handlers ([exn:fail? (lambda (e) '())]) guard silently swallows the resulting 'module->imports: unknown module in the current namespace' error as 'no further imports', causing the transitive walk to silently truncate one level early for every relatively-required module (the majority of this codebase's import graph). Verified the truncation affects even racket/contract's own internal submodules when reached via a relative chain. This undermines the item's core claim of being 'the first item to assert it TRANSITIVELY' for any banned import nested 2+ relative-require-hops deep.

MAJOR: The spec's own mandated drift-detection check (inject racket/tcp and confirm detection) was only verified at 0-1 relative-require hops in the spec's research transcript and in this review's re-verification. A banned module nested 2+ hops deep was not successfully tested end-to-end (blocked by the 749-collision compile failure) but is very plausibly missed given the demonstrated truncation. The AC and Manual Validation Checklist must require testing at 2+ hops to actually validate the transitive claim.

MODERATE: The first Acceptance Criteria bullet ('raco make ... compiles with NO name clashes error') is demonstrably false as worded -- the actual first failure mode is a different error string ('module: identifier already required'), so a check that greps for the literal substring 'name clashes' would produce a false negative (build fails, but not with that string). Recommend rewording to an outcome-based check (exit code 0) instead of a cause-based one (absence of one specific error substring).

MINOR (confirmed correct, no action needed): the internal-only-binding curation test section (split-loose/h-opt/put! from spec-2025-11-25.rkt, url-elicitation-data?/unsupported-version-data? from errors.rkt) is concrete, accurate, and independently verified against the real files -- this section meets item 007's rigor bar as-is.

MINOR (confirmed correct, no action needed): the module->imports single-return-value pitfall warning is accurate -- (define-values (imps _) (module->imports m)) does raise an arity-mismatch error in this Racket 8.18 install, exactly as the spec warns.

SUGGESTED: add explicit scope-boundary notes for (a) test-submodule-only banned requires (out of scope, module->imports won't see them) and (b) circular barrel requires (N/A, the two barrels form a one-directional DAG) -- both are correctly out of scope but left implicit rather than stated.
